questions.q: In a typical DNS lookup, which server does the resolver query first?
Which DNS record type maps a name to an IPv4 address?
Which record is used for reverse DNS lookups?
What is the main purpose of TTL in DNS?
questions.a: To tell resolvers how long they may cache a record

questions.q: Why can a CNAME not be placed at the zone apex?
questions.a: Because the apex must also hold other records like NS and SOA.

questions.q: What mechanism authorizes mail senders for a domain?
What is the correct order of servers in recursive resolution?

questions.a: Cryptographic authenticity and integrity for DNS data.

questions.q: Why do low TTL values help during a migration?
questions.a: They reduce cache lifetime so clients pick up new answers faster.
Serving different DNS answers based on where the query comes from.

questions.q: Why is DNS amplification effective for attackers?
questions.a: Small spoofed requests can trigger much larger replies from many servers.

questions.q: What is anycast used for in DNS?
questions.a: To serve the same IP from many locations and route clients to a nearby instance.

questions.q: Why does DNS use both recursion and delegation?
questions.a: Recursion simplifies clients, while delegation distributes authority across zones.

questions.q: What tradeoff comes with a very low TTL on critical records?
questions.a: Faster changes, but more cache misses and higher query volume.

questions.q: Why can DNS failures break a service even if the service itself is healthy?
questions.a: Clients cannot discover or refresh the correct endpoint if name resolution fails.

questions.q: Why are TXT records commonly used for verification and policy?
questions.a: They are flexible and easy to add without changing the service protocol itself.
It reduces repeated lookups for names that do not exist, saving load on authoritative servers.

questions.q: Why is DNS still a bottleneck in many systems?
questions.a: Because every new hostname or cache miss adds latency before the application can connect.
